topic: Netflix recs — needs something light after 12 hour days

turns.content: okay I actually have a free evening tonight for the first time in literally weeks and I want to watch something good on Netflix. I need recommendations but like actually good ones not the stuff that comes up on the trending page

A rare free evening deserves a good pick! Before I throw out suggestions — what are you in the mood for? Something light and easy to switch off to, or something gripping that you'll want to binge? And are there any genres you tend to avoid?

light. like genuinely light. I've been doing 12 hour days at work and my brain cannot handle anything intense or dark or emotionally draining right now. I tried watching a thriller last week and I fell asleep in 15 minutes loool. not because it was boring but because I was that tired

Ha — falling asleep during a thriller is peak exhaustion. Okay, light and low-effort it is. Here are some options depending on what flavour of light you want:

**Feel-good comedy:**
- *Heartstopper* — if you haven't seen it, it's incredibly wholesome. Low stakes, great characters, you'll smile the whole time
- *Never Have I Ever* — teen comedy but genuinely funny, diverse cast, easy to watch
- *The Good Place* — smart but not demanding, very rewatchable

**Comforting reality TV:**
- *Somebody Feed Phil* — food travel show with the most wholesome host alive. Zero drama, just good food and genuine human connection
- *The Great British Bake Off* — the TV equivalent of a warm hug

**Easy drama (low-stakes, pretty to look at):**
- *Emily in Paris* — it's ridiculous and it knows it, but it's fun and the outfits are good
- *Bridgerton* — costume drama, romance, very easy to get absorbed in

Anything jumping out?

I've seen Heartstopper and Never Have I Ever already!! both so good. Bridgerton I watched season 1 and liked it but haven't gone back to the later seasons. Emily in Paris is too cringe for me I can't do it loool. the food show sounds interesting though what's that about

Somebody Feed Phil is basically Phil Rosenthal (the guy who created Everybody Loves Raymond) travelling to different cities and eating his way through them. But what makes it different from other food shows is that he's genuinely the most enthusiastic, warm person — he talks to everyone, he's interested in the culture behind the food, and he has this infectious joy about everything he eats.

There's zero competition, zero drama, zero manufactured tension. It's just a nice man eating nice food in beautiful places. It's the most relaxing show on Netflix, honestly. Each episode is a different city — Bangkok, Lisbon, Montreal, etc.

Perfect for when your brain is fried and you just want to feel good.

okay sold. I'm starting that tonight. is there anything similar but like with a bit more substance? for when I have a weekend free and want something I can properly get into but still not dark or heavy

For a weekend watch with more substance but still not heavy:

- *Schitt's Creek* — starts out as a comedy about rich people losing everything, becomes one of the most heartfelt shows ever made. The character development is genuinely beautiful. Six seasons, all on Netflix.

- *One Day* — the Netflix adaptation. It's a love story spanning years and it's emotional but not heavy in a draining way. More bittersweet than dark.

- *Beef* — this one's a bit edgier but it's darkly funny, not depressing. Two strangers have a road rage incident and it spirals. Incredible acting.

- *Abbott Elementary* — technically not Netflix (it's on other platforms) but if you have access, it's the best workplace comedy in years. Think The Office but in a struggling school. Very warm.

Schitt's Creek would be my top rec for a weekend binge honestly. It's the kind of show that makes you genuinely sad when it's over.
I've heard SO many people talk about Schitt's Creek and I've never started it. I think it's the name that put me off initially loool. but everyone says it's amazing so maybe that's my weekend project

The name definitely puts people off — it sounds like it's going to be crude humour but it really isn't. It's written by Eugene Levy and his son Daniel Levy, and it's one of those shows where the first few episodes are setting up the characters as shallow and ridiculous, and then it slowly, quietly becomes the most generous and loving show on television.

Fair warning: the first 3-4 episodes are a bit slow because you're meant to find the characters annoying. Stick with it. By mid-season one you'll be hooked, and by season three you'll be emotionally attached to all of them.

It also holds up really well on rewatch, which is a sign of quality writing.
okay you've convinced me. Somebody Feed Phil tonight, Schitt's Creek this weekend. what about something I can watch in like 20 minute episodes? for lunch breaks at work when I need a mental reset

Perfect lunch break format — short episodes, no cliffhangers, you can dip in and out:

- *Brooklyn Nine-Nine* — if you haven't seen it, police comedy but very lighthearted. Andy Samberg. 22-minute episodes, completely self-contained. Easy to watch one and go back to work.

- *Derry Girls* — Northern Irish teenagers in the 90s. Absolutely hilarious, each episode is standalone-ish, and it's only 3 short seasons so no commitment anxiety.

- *Kim's Convenience* — Canadian-Korean family comedy. Warm, funny, 22-minute episodes. Very underrated.

- *Nailed It!* — baking competition where bad bakers try to recreate elaborate cakes and fail spectacularly. Each episode is 30 minutes and it's genuinely the funniest thing. No skill required to enjoy it.

Derry Girls would be my pick for you — it's chaotic in the best way and the episodes are the perfect length.

DERRY GIRLS. yes I watched season 1 ages ago and forgot to finish it! I loved it. okay that's my lunch break show sorted. right I now have a full viewing schedule loool. tonight Phil, lunch breaks Derry Girls, weekend Schitt's Creek. my free time is booked

A well-curated viewing schedule is genuinely important self-care when you're working 12-hour days. You've got comfort food TV for tonight, chaotic comedy for lunch resets, and a proper heartfelt binge for the weekend. Solid lineup across the board.

Enjoy Phil tonight — grab some good food to eat while you watch, it makes the experience even better. And let Schitt's Creek sneak up on you this weekend. You'll know you're hooked when you start talking like Moira Rose without realising it.

loool I already do accents when I'm tired so Moira Rose is going to be dangerous. okay I'm going to make some food and settle in. genuinely thank you, this is exactly what I needed — someone to just tell me what to watch so I don't spend my entire free evening scrolling through the Netflix menu and then going to bed having watched nothing

The Netflix scroll paralysis is so real — spending 45 minutes choosing and then being too tired to actually watch anything. That's why having a plan matters. You've got yours now, so close the browse screen and just hit play on Somebody Feed Phil episode one. No second-guessing.

Enjoy your evening — you've earned it after those 12-hour days.
